entry.title: Re: Event‑driven architecture
entry.content: A good approach is to keep Event Mesh focused on reliable event routing and let AI services handle the intelligence layer. Use clear event schemas, avoid sending unnecessary data, and make sure AI decisions are traceable before triggering business actions in S/4HANA. For large-scale utility scenarios, monitoring, retry handling, and human approval paths for critical actions are just as important as the AI model itself.

entry.title: Starting Nov 2026, Town Name and Country are conditional mandatory fields for Wire payments,
entry.content: Starting Nov 2026, Town Name and Country are conditional mandatory fields for Wire payments, meaning if address is provided, then “Town Name” and “Country” become mandatory fields, as attached the example to the case.

entry.title: Clarification on Personalization Migration from SAP Fiori 2 to SAP Fiori 3 - SAP UI_758 SP0005
entry.content:
Hi, SAP Team, 

We are currently migrating our SAP Fiori Launchpad from SAP Fiori 2 to SAP Fiori 3 (Spaces & Pages / My Home).

We would like to clarify the expected behavior regarding user personalization migration.

Specifically, we would like confirmation on the following:

  1. Which user personalization settings are expected to be retained after the migration (for example, theme, user defaults, favorites, tile order, and home page layout)?
  2. Are existing Fiori 2 Groups and user-personalized tile arrangements automatically migrated to Spaces & Pages or My Home?

    Could you please confirm whether SAP provides any SAP-supported migration tool or standard procedure to migrate user personalization from SAP Fiori 2 (Groups) to SAP Fiori 3 (Spaces & Pages/My Home)

entry.title: AI-Assisted Journal Upload
entry.content:
I am Betty Guedez, Product & Engineering Solution Architect at SAP Regional Implementation Group RIG. I am coming back to this community with an SAP strategic topic: Business AI.

 

            More specifically, Autonomous Finance. AI is the cornerstone of SAP Autonomous Enterprise offering, that revolutionize the way we support our customers businesses: Innovations that develop state-of the-art tools and agents to automate tasks, where humans can be released of cumbersome tasks to focus on business intelligence and financial engineering to maximize benefits with real-time value.

           

            As part of the SAP Autonomous Enterprise, SAP Autonomous Finance is built out of Joule Assistants that orchestrate all the Finance agents that support this business process. AI-assisted Journal Upload is part of Financial Closing Assistant, that drives the full close lifecycle across posting, accruals, journal validation, reconciliations, and so on. The major goal of Financial Closing Assistant is to reducing closing time and streamlines the reporting associated to closing.

 

 

Figure 1: SAP Finance Assistants-Financial Closing Assistant

 

            For years, SAP has developed solutions that enable customers to perform specific business tasks which, while executed outside the ERP system and often requiring significant time and effort, provide critical input for gaining a complete view of the business. Tasks that, if not performed, can disrupt business operations and compromise data visibility. Uploading journal entries is one of them. It is either a periodic or frequent activity or both, in every landscape: satellite systems, legacies, integrations that support the operation in a way or another, that feedback the ERP with transactions that must be posted to keep the accounting books updated.

 

            Many of us have used the different ways to upload journal entries to the ERP: The traditional file (we used to call it "flat") with a fixed structure of columns with specific format. Another is the journal entry template, for postings based on your needs, where fields and values can be selected to produce a tailored-specific template for postings, taking out non-relevant fields and focusing only on the desired ones. This template can also be shared and with pre-defined level of use (group, company, user). Please look at Fiori app Manage Journal Entry Templates (F3803)documentation to know of this, in case you are interested. Both have been extremely useful tools, widely used by customers and partners.

            Month-end journal entries upload is more needed than ever. Increasingly complex system landscapes, new integration points, constantly evolving business models, and the ever-changing business environment demonstrate that, if this process can be executed autonomously with minimal supervision, the resulting efficiency gains and cost savings can make a significant contribution to the organization's value creation efforts. 

What is AI-Assisted Journal Upload?

 

            As period-end journal entries are necessary for period closing, accounting teams are spending considerable time in curating data as per the policies and reporting needs. Through new SAP Fiori apps with Generative AI capabilities embedded, AI-assisted Journal Upload in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition accelerates the creation and the upload of period-end manual journal entries.

            AI-assisted Journal Upload is a Financial AI feature to speed-up the creation and the upload of period-end manual journal entries.

            With AI-assisted Journal Upload, accountants can:

  • Assign a guidance document (written in natural language) for posting to the journal upload case
  • Create journal upload cases
  • Generate journal posting proposals according to the assigned guidance
  • Validate the posting proposals before posting and
  • Post the posting proposal as journals

 

What are typical business cases for AI-Assisted Journal Upload?

            Business scenarios best suited for AI-assisted Journal Upload include recurring general ledger journal entries with a high volume of records and similar information requirements, making them ideal candidates for this autonomous feature. Other excellent use cases include large volumes of prepaid expenses, lease or rental in advance-payments, and customer prepayments, all of which can be efficiently processed using AI-assisted Journal Upload.

Is AI-assisted Journal Upload intended for large companies only?

 

            Not at all. Regardless of their size, Companies often pay for services they will receive in the future. Small and large businesses work with prepaid expenses frequently. It is a way to secure better terms, benefit from early-payment discounts, and take advantage of periods of stronger cash flow while ensuring futre obligations are covered.

 

            When the volume of prepaid operations is high, as they happen in advance, a proper tracking is crucial for financial control and compliance, to record them when is right to do it (in the posting period when each service is received). Plus, a posting template easy to use and populate to post to the right accounting objects, is invaluable for saving time and reducing the risk of errors during the financial closing. The easiest, the better. These are some of the advantages of using AI-assisted Journal Upload.

 

            In the graphic below, we find some interesting figures about uploading journal entries for a business (complexity varies in each case). It can be found on SAP Discovery Center - S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition - AI-Assisted Journal Upload:

 

 

Figure 2: AI-assisted Journal Upload - Estimated benefits

            Technically speaking, AI-Assisted Journal Upload is an embedded Intelligent Scenario delivered via SAP's Intelligent Scenario Lifecycle Management (ISLM) framework, using scenario FIN_INTLGNT_JRNLUPLD. It is natively embedded within SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, with the underlying AI service provisioned through SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) as a backend infrastructure integration — not a side-by-side deployment.

What are the specifications of AI-Assisted Journal Upload?

  • Identifier: J247
  • AI Type: AI Feature
  • Support Component: FI-GL-AI-IJU
  • Minimun Required System Version: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2023 FPS03
  • Required Roles & Authorizations:
    • BTP: Global Account Administrator role, assigned via Security > Role Collections in the BTP Cockpit
    • S/4HANA: ISLM Administrator role to manage intelligent scenario activation
  • BTP Subaccount & Entitlement Requirements:
    • A valid BTP subaccount must exist and be accessible
    • The AI service entitlement must be available and assigned to the relevant subaccount prior to service instance creation

 

What do we need to start using AI-assisted Journal Upload?

 

 

            3 easy steps to determine AI-assisted Journal Upload readiness:

  • Check 1 - To verify the Scenario Status in Intelligent Scenario Management (F4470)  Fiori app, you need to confirm that FIN_INTLGNT_JRNLUPLD shows a Published status with no errors in the ISLM app
 
 

 

 

Figure 3: Intelligent Scenario Management

 
 

 

Figure 4: Manage Journal Posting Policies
Check 2 - Confirm that Fiori App Manage Journal Posting Policies (F8539) is available in Fiori Launchpad (FLP). Check role authorizations and catalogs if it is not visible

 

 
 

 

Figure 5: Manage Journal Upload Cases
Check 3 - Confirm that Fiori App Manage Journal Upload Cases (F8538) is available in Fiori Launchpad (FLP). Same considerations as F8539 here.

 

            Also, it is always good to make sure that content in SAP Note 3573144 AI-assisted Journal Upload in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private has been implemented in the system.

            Let me share the link to my blog post AI-assisted Journal Upload - How to use it 

            I hope you find this insightful. In my next blog, I will go step by step on how to use AI-assisted Journal Upload.

Thank you,

Brought to you by the SAP S/4HANA RIG team

entry.title: AI-Assisted Journal Upload - How to use it
entry.content:
I am Betty Guedez, Product & Engineering Solution Architect at SAP Regional Implementation Group RIG. I am coming back with the second part of my blog post AI-assisted Journal Upload . This time, how to use it.

 

            Remember that AI-Assisted Journal Upload is an AI feature, part of the SAP Autonomous Finance offering.

 

            We also know that SAP Autonomous Finance is one of the Joule Assistants in SAP Autonomous Enterprise platform, and it is there to that orchestrate all the Finance agents that support this business process. AI-assisted Journal Upload is part of Financial Closing Assistant, that drives the full close lifecycle across posting, accruals, journal validation, reconciliations, and so on.

 

           

Start using AI-assisted Journal Upload

 

            AI-assisted Journal Upload requires a couple of IMG-customizing-related activities in the backend, related to the category and subcategory of the journal entries you will post. Following the path Financial Accounting => Financial Accounting Global Settings => Document => Intelligent Journal Uploads, you will see the two activities, Define Journal Upload Case Categories and Define Journal Upload Case Subcategories. Together, they both serve to give a classification to the expenses that better explain your postings.

Define Journal Upload Case Categories:

Figure 1: Define Journal Upload Case Categories

 Define Journal Upload Case Subcategories:

Figure 2: Define Journal Upload Case Subcategories


            Next, to download and adapt the policy template, we access the Fiori App Manage Journal Posting Policies (F8539). With this app, we set up all the relevant fields and the natural language instructions to populate the fields on the template.

 
 

 

Figure 3: Manage Journal Posting Policies - Initial Screen


            Click on Download Template, select the format (Spreadsheet, PDF) and OK. (I feel more comfortable with the excel file, so I chose the first one).

 
 

 

Figure 4: Manage Journal Posting Policies - Template Downloaded

            Follow the guidelines provided and update the Input instructions that the feature  is going to use to handle the data in the input file. It is important to bear in mind:

  • For now, Cardinality - Optional/Mandatory (O/M)- field is not required. It will be supported in future releases, so you may leave it blank.
  • The file name format must be "Name", followed by a "_", followed by the "Language Code".ext (xslx, pdf). Supported language codes are EN (English), DE (German), ES (Spanish), FR (French), PT (Portuguese), JA (Japanese), ZH (Simplified Chinese). Example: "Policy Document 2026_EN.xlsx".
  • Make sure that the fields referenced in the Input Instruction of a field in the policy document, are columns in the input data file, for example:
 
 

 

Figure 5: Manage Journal Posting Policies - Template - Input Instruction examples

            Based on the instruction for field WRBTR, there should be a column labeled "Amount" with values in the input file.


            And now, let's work with the policy in the system:

Figure 6: Manage Journal Posting Policies - Policy Creation

Case category and Subcategory: According to customizing mentioned above

Description: Short Text about the policy purpose (max 50 characters)

Valid to: Defaulted to 12/31/9999

Valid from: Starting validity date of the policy. If there is an existing policy of the same Category and Subcategory, with Valid from date prior or equal to this, the system will raise an error, and the policy cannot be saved. The policy must start after the starting date of any previously created policy of this service.

 
 

 

Figure 7: Manage Journal Posting Policies - Policy Activation

 

 

            Once created, the policy needs to be Activated.

            Now, it is the time to upload the input data to post the journal entries.

            We designed a simplified excel file that contains the data of the charges received for shuttle services for 3 employees, to create the postings for the expenses charged for this service:

Figure 8: Manage Journal Upload Cases: Sample input data


            To do that, open the Fiori app Manage Journal Upload Cases (F8538) and hit Create:

Figure 9: Manage Journal Upload Cases: Updating the Posting Proposal

            Once created, a Proposal must be generated:

Figure 10: Manage Journal Upload Cases: Generating the Posting Proposal

            Proposal Validation:

Figure 11: Manage Journal Upload Cases: Validating the Posting Proposal

            And proposal finally to be posted:

Figure 12: Manage Upload Cases: Posting the Proposal

           

            Let's display the postings in the Fiori app Manage Journal Entries (F0717A):

 

Figure 13: Manage Journal Entries: Displaying the Postings

 

            The posted document SA 1000000006 shows that rides of the three employees using the shuttle bus service in the second week of June 2026 were posted to the right posting period (06/2026), crediting the prepaid expense (which is currently a right, in consequence) for $264,00 USD.

            AI-assisted Journal Upload is a key component of the SAP Autonomous Enterprise offering and provides an easy, straightforward process to generate postings in S/4HANA from external sources, as part of the month-end closing, releasing the business experts from spending lots of time and effort fulfilling templates that can be well known, but still somewhat error-prone.

            Before closing, let me share two more useful resources with you.

SAP AI Services in Discovery Center

AI-assisted Journal Upload in help.sap.com

            I hope you find this insightful.

Thank you,

Brought to you by the SAP S/4HANA RIG team

entry.title: Re: Windows Upgrade - Connection/Driver Issues with OLAP/...
entry.content:
As you've found in Note 2970913, SDK for SAP ASE 16.x no longer provides or supports the Sybase OLE DB Provider, ODBC is suggested to be used instead.

To continue using SSAS Multidimensional I'm afraid you would need to consider introducing a SQL Server staging database and loading ASE data through supported ASE connectivity methods like ODBC.

Or consult Microsoft see if there are any other workaround or alternative software to use.

entry.title: Re: Restrict Project Creation in CJ20N to Project Templat...
entry.content:
Recommended Approach

Step 1: Define template projects with fixed WBS structures.

Step 2: Configure user statuses + BSX to block unwanted actions.

Step 3: Implement a validation exit (CNEX0015) to enforce:

  • Project creation only via copy from template.
  • No new WBS creation/deletion.
  • No hierarchy changes.

Step 4: Allow updates to master data fields (dates, responsibilities) by not restricting those.

This hybrid approach (standard + enhancement) ensures governance while keeping flexibility for operational data maintenance.

entry.title: Re: Error FZ 349: Vendor && is contained in propo...
entry.content: SAP standard does not allow blocking only specific line items in F110. The block is always at the account level. The recommended approach is to use payment block indicators or payment methods to control item selection, rather than trying to change the blocking logic itself.

entry.title: Re: A custom field was added to BSEG, but it cannot be ed...
entry.content:
In ECC, BSEG is the central line item table for accounting documents. It was the “single source of truth” for line items, so custom fields were directly appended and editable. In S/4HANA, BSEG is no longer the primary line item table. The Universal Journal ACDOCA consolidates FI, CO, AA, ML, and other components into one line item table. BSEG still exists, but it is now a compatibility view (technically a proxy) rather than the main storage table. Because of this you cannot simply append custom fields to BSEG and edit them via OB32. OB32 still exists, but it controls field status for certain classic transactions, not for Universal Journal extensibility.

Use the Fiori app to add custom fields to ACDOCA. These fields can be enabled for posting, reporting, and analytics.

entry.title: GRC Tuesdays: Moving Beyond Automation - Agentic AI for Risk, Compliance, and Audit
entry.content:
Risk, Compliance and Audit teams have long operated under competing pressures: mounting regulatory demands, siloed data, and manual processes that consume time better spent on judgment. Traditional GRC automation isn't enough anymore. Traditional workflow automation follows predefined rules, while risks evolve faster than those rules can be updated. At the same time, compliance monitoring is becoming increasingly continuous, requiring systems that can analyze, reason, and act.

Agentic AI provides the missing layer between traditional automation and truly continuous governance.

 

The shape of Autonomous GRC

 

The shift from reactive to proactive compliance isn't simply a technology upgrade, it's a fundamental change in what's possible.

When AI agents handle the repetitive, high-volume, time-sensitive work of GRC, human expertise gets applied where it creates the most value: judgment, escalation, and strategic risk management.

Unlike earlier automation tools that required rigid rules and predefined workflows, AI agents reason, adapt, and act across complex processes—from detecting anomalies to drafting audit-ready documents. For GRC, agents don't just speed up existing work; they make continuously vigilant oversight possible at a scale no human team can match.

As a result, we're building a suite of AI agents for SAP GRC to do exactly that—coordinated by a central orchestrator called the Governance Assistant.

 

The Governance Assistant: your GRC command center

 

The Governance Assistant serves as the AI control center for GRC operations, serving as the intelligence layer that coordinates specialized agents, enterprise risk data, compliance activities, controls, and audit execution

It connects Governance, Risk, and Compliance teams to AI agents that monitor risks, streamline compliance workflows, surface control exceptions, and turn complex governance data into clear, actionable insights – faster than traditional manual approaches allow.

Whether identifying open control deficiencies, summarizing emerging regulatory changes, or highlighting unresolved audit issues, the Governance Assistant turns fragmented governance data into actionable insight.

What makes the Governance Assistant distinctive is its built-in human-in-the-loop design. AI and people work together – the assistant accelerates analysis and surfaces recommendations, while your team retains control over decisions that matter.

This balance means faster authorization requests, sharper visibility into regulatory changes, improved audit quality, and quicker remediation when issues arise. In practice, use cases span from breaking down siloed compliance monitoring to automating evidence collection for audits, optimizing access request workflows, and surfacing control irregularities before they escalate.

 

Internal Audit Agent: from announcements to audit programs in minutes [Planned for Q4 2026]

 

Audit lifecycles are effort-intensive, language-dependent, and time-consuming to coordinate. The Internal Audit Agent reimagines this from the ground up and boosts audit productivity.

When an audit announcement arrives—in any language—the Internal Audit Agent automatically detects the language, translates the content, classifies the audit type, assigns the right team, and sets up the audit plan. It then generates a complete work program from the attached questionnaire, matching incoming questions semantically against a growing knowledge base of prior answers and clustering them into scopes and question procedures. What used to take days – intake, translation, clustering, and team assignment – now happens in minutes.

The agent supports any audit type: tax, compliance, IT, and more. It even drafts audit reports and response letters automatically, translating back into the original announcement language.

Every completed audit enriches the knowledge base, so matching accuracy and work program quality improve continuously. For global teams, language barriers disappear. For auditors, that means less time on logistics and more time on the questions that genuinely require human judgment.

For sneak peak, here’s an early overview video:

 

Integrity Screening Insights Agent: transparent, auditable screening [Planned for Q4 2026]

 

Screening business partners against sanctions lists, politically exposed persons databases, adverse media, and state-owned enterprise registers is a time-intensive compliance obligation. And the consequences of errors can be severe!

The Integrity Screening Insights Agent automates this process within SAP Business Integrity Screening – and more SAP solutions will be connected in the future to provide transparency and explainability to compliance teams.

It collects and aggregates evidence across multiple data sources automatically, using configurable risk dimensions and country or industry-specific analysis profiles. Each screening result comes with a transparent confidence score that shows exactly how each dimension contributed: weighted evidence sources, freshness indicators, and conflict detection across sources:

This matters for audit trails. Compliance analysts don't just get a result, they get an explanation. They can see why a score is high, which sources drove it, and whether the underlying data is current. That transparency makes AI-generated results defensible, internally but also to regulators.

Critically, the Integrity Screening Insights Agent works alongside existing address list screening rather than replacing it, adding intelligence without disrupting established compliance controls.

 

Compliance Agent: control validation at scale [Planned for Q4 2026]

 

Traditional compliance monitoring works in cycles with periodic reviews, point-in-time audits, and after-the-fact testing. The gaps between checkpoints are where risk lives.

The Compliance Agent changes the operating model entirely. Instead of periodic testing, it continuously monitors, tests, and validates internal controls – automating the manual Test of Control Effectiveness process and executing test cases for manual controls automatically.

Repetitive monitoring tasks move off your team's plate, freeing capacity for higher-value analysis. Bottlenecks in compliance workflows disappear because continuous automation doesn't wait for the next review cycle.

The cost equation also shifts: by demonstrating ongoing compliance monitoring to regulators, organizations can improve audit readiness and reduce effort associated with external audits, and lower insurance premiums and liability exposure through stronger demonstrated compliance.

Beyond cost, there's a risk quality dimension: consistent and systematic testing can reduce human variability and improve coverage, anomalies surface early before they escalate, and compliance policies are enforced consistently across all business units. The result is a documented, continuous evidence trail that makes every future audit faster and more straightforward to close.

 

And more to come!

 

This is only the beginning. Additional agents are already being planned across the broader GRC landscape. Looking ahead, Agentic AI will also reshape Enterprise Risk Management. Future capabilities could continuously monitor key risk indicators, detect emerging risks from internal and external signals, suggest risk assessments, and help risk managers understand how evolving threats impact strategic objectives.

 

Trusted and Responsible AI at SAP: What GRC Professionals Need to Know

 

SAP defines Responsible AI around three explicit pillars — ethics, security, and compliance — and states that every AI system it creates must be grounded in ethical principles, comply with applicable law (including the EU AI Act), and maintain technical robustness and data security. This is not aspirational language: it is governed through an SAP Global AI Ethics Policy that is binding for all SAP employees involved in AI development, deployment, use, and sale.

Organizations that put trusted and responsible AI automation at the center of their GRC operations can experience significant benefits:

While organizations remain accountable for governance and compliance decisions, AI agents can significantly reduce the operational burden associated with monitoring, testing, and documentation.

Provided they are adequately governed. Agentic AI in GRC requires clear governance boundaries. Organizations must determine which activities agents can execute autonomously, which require approval, how evidence is retained, and how decisions remain explainable and auditable.

With this in place, organizations will shift compliance from reactive cycle-based work to continuous, AI-assisted oversight and lay the foundation for continuous assurance across risk, controls, compliance, and audit activities

The Governance Assistant orchestrates a set of purpose-built AI agents with Audit Agent, Integrity Insights Agent, and Compliance Agent already planned and more to come, each targeting a specific high-effort, high-risk area of the GRC lifecycle. Together, they shift compliance from reactive cycle-based work to continuous, AI-assisted oversight.

What about you, what is your strategy to alleviate workload from your GRC teams? I look forward to reading your thoughts and comments on this blog.

And if you are interested in learning more about SAP solutions for Governance, Risk, and Compliance, feel free to fill-in the demo request form!

entry.title: Re: Some assets are showing zero value in asset master re...
entry.content:
For a detailed explanation of this issue, you can refer to SAP KBA 1886232 - Error AA632 occurs when posting retirement.

This KBA clarifies that error AA632 is triggered when an asset’s net book value falls below its scrap value during a retirement posting.

If your business requirement is to fully retire an asset to a net book value of zero,  the best practice is to create a new depreciation key by copying the existing key YLIN (in transaction AFAMA). When configuring this new key, ensure that you do not assign a cut-off value key. This will allow the net book value to be reduced to zero.

This approach is strongly recommended because it will not impact any other existing assets that currently use the YLIN key. For this reason, you should avoid modifying the original YLIN key directly.

entry.title: Re: How to Prevent Duplicate GST Numbers in Customer and...
entry.content: Hi, Thank you for your guidance and for sharing the relevant KBAs. The proposed solution worked perfectly for our requirement. We implemented the configuration using SSCUI 102415 and SSCUI 103601, and the system is now correctly preventing the creation of Business Partners with duplicate GST Registration Numbers. However, we have encountered another scenario and would appreciate your guidance. For unregistered vendors, our business team currently selects Tax Number Category = IN3 (GST Registration Number) and enters "NA" as the GST number, since the vendor is not GST registered. As all unregistered vendors are maintained with the value "NA", the duplicate validation is triggered, preventing the creation of additional unregistered vendors. Could you please advise on the SAP standard approach for this scenario? Should the IN3 (GST Registration Number) category be left blank for unregistered vendors? Is there any standard configuration or recommended business practice to handle multiple GST-unregistered vendors without triggering duplicate validation? Is there an alternative tax number category or indicator that should be used instead of maintaining "NA"? Looking forward to your guidance. Thank you once again for your support.

entry.title: Re: BTP + Joule integration
entry.content:
Your case needs a 'reactive' agent to monitor the events (signals) from S/4HANA. Joule in its current form is more of a 'reactive' agent orchestrator i.e. they must be triggered by humans.

There is a category of 'reactive' agents called - 'Observer Agents' which can silently watch S/4HANA's billing-events or grid-telemetry stream, and uses tools for anomaly detection, prediction, root-cause analysis etc. The solution may require some intermediary components which can filter/aggregate/summarize the event stream (such as dropping non-critical ones, while collecting events by time or count), before the AI Agent can be triggered for analysis and eventually allowing it to take an action such as adjusting billing rules, alerting humans.

entry.title: Duplicate GST Validation Triggered for Unregistered Vendors Using "NA" as GST Number(Public Cloud Sy
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

Based on the recommendations provided in the SAP Community, we have implemented duplicate GST number validation by configuring the following:

  • SSCUI 102415 – Maintain Tax Number Categories

  • SSCUI 103601 – Change Message Control for Duplicate Tax Numbers

The duplicate validation is working as expected. The system prevents the creation of a new Business Partner when the GST Registration Number (Tax Category IN3) already exists for another Customer or Supplier.

However, we are facing an issue with unregistered vendors.

Current Business Process

For unregistered vendors, the business team:

  1. Creates a new Supplier Business Partner.

  2. Selects Tax Number Category = IN3 (GST Registration Number).

  3. Enters "NA" in the GST Number field, since the vendor is not GST registered.

As all unregistered vendors are maintained with the value "NA", the system considers it as a duplicate GST number and prevents the creation of subsequent unregistered vendors.

Our Question

What is the SAP standard approach for handling GST-unregistered vendors in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition?

Specifically:

  • Is it recommended not to maintain Tax Category IN3 for GST-unregistered vendors?

  • Is there any standard configuration or business practice to allow multiple unregistered vendors without triggering duplicate validation?

  • Is there any alternative tax number category or indicator that should be used instead of maintaining "NA" under Tax Category IN3?

We would appreciate your guidance on the recommended SAP standard practice for this scenario.

Thank you in advance.

entry.title: Clarification on Standard Processing of Multiple DELFOR IDocs
entry.content:
The sender system (Infineon) and the receiver system (Bosch) are both SAP systems.

Infineon sends Supplier Confirmations for the same Scheduling Agreement in two separate DELFOR IDocs (one containing Firm schedule lines and another containing Forecast schedule lines) in DELFOR D.97A/D.04A format.

We would like to understand the standard SAP behavior for processing these multiple inbound DELFOR IDocs. Specifically, how should SAP process Supplier Confirmations received in separate IDocs for the same Scheduling Agreement.

Expectation:

 

Is there any standard SAP functionality to merge or aggregate multiple inbound DELFOR IDocs into a single Supplier Confirmation before updating the Scheduling Agreement (EKES)? If yes, please provide the standard program, function module, class, or processing logic responsible for this scenario.

 

If no such standard functionality exists, please advise the recommended SAP standard approach for processing Supplier Confirmations received in multiple DELFOR IDocs for the same Scheduling Agreement.

entry.title: Title: [S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition] Automatic Payments (F0770): how to post the payment directly W
entry.content:
Title: [S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition] Automatic Payments (F0770): how to post the payment directly WITHOUT creating a payment order

Environment
- SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (Release: <fill in, e.g. 2602>)
- App: Manage Automatic Payments – Fiori ID F0770

Current behavior
When I execute a payment run in Manage Automatic Payments (F0770), the supplier open items are NOT cleared at execution time. Instead they seem to be reserved as a payment order.
As a result, when I try to clear the same items manually in Post Outgoing Payments (F1612), the action is disabled and I get:
"Item(s) 1 are part of a payment order and cannot be selected for clearing"
(This matches KBA 3503165.)
So my current payment method appears to be running in "payment order" mode.

What I want to achieve
I want the payment run to DIRECTLY post the payment document at execution time and immediately clear the supplier open item (posting the offset to the bank clearing / bank sub account) — i.e. I do NOT want a payment order to be created.

What I have checked so far (for reference only – not sure this is correct)
I suspect the "Payment order only" flag at the country-level payment method settings is involved (possibly SSCUI 101972 – Set Up Payment Methods for Each Country/Region for Payment Transactions), but I am not sure whether this flag is editable / the correct lever in Public Edition.

My question
Could someone provide the concrete, step-by-step configuration procedure to achieve DIRECT posting (no payment order) in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, WITH the specific SSCUI IDs and Fiori App IDs for each step?
Specifically:
1. Exactly which setting needs to be changed, in which SSCUI (please include the SSCUI ID)?
2. What follow-on settings must be maintained (e.g. document type for payment in posting details)?
3. Any impact I should be aware of on Electronic Bank Statement clearing (since payment-order-number based matching would no longer apply)?
4. How to handle open items that are already blocked by an existing payment order?

Thanks in advance!

entry.title: Re: Error while changing depreciation start date in asset...
entry.content:
Dear All!

When you change the depreciation start a recalculation of the asset values is being performed for all open fiscal years in asset accounting (last closed fiscal year can be seen in table T093B field ABGJA. The error you receive is usually cause by the fact that asset fiscal year is open and the posting periods are close in FI-GL transaction OB52. 

You should first close asset accounting and than the posting periods in order to avoid inconsistencies.

Please read SAP Note 3335047 Closing FI-GL before FI-AA (SAP S/4HANA only) ir

619969 Closing FI-GL before FI-AA

Best regards

Werner 

SAP Support

entry.title: Re: Duplicate GST Validation Triggered for Unregistered V...
entry.content: IN3 is specifically for GST Registration Numbers. If a vendor is not GST‑registered, the recommended approach is to leave IN3 blank. Entering “NA” is interpreted as an actual tax number, so the system enforces uniqueness. Having said that, it is not recommended to maintain IN3 with “NA” for unregistered vendors. The standard practice is to leave IN3 blank unless a valid GSTIN exists, and if you need to track unregistered status, use a separate tax category or BP attribute.

entry.title: Re: Change field selection in "Create Customer Project"
entry.content:
Hi @yKim,

Our UI colleague confirmed that the Cost Center field is rendered as a sap.m.Select control, which is a standard HTML-style dropdown list. It has no search or filter capability.

What you observed with English characters is not a search function. It is standard Select keyboard navigation: pressing a key on your keyboard moves the highlight to the first item in the list that begins with that character. For example, pressing "C" jumps to "Csltg Unit A (JP)". This is a browser-level behavior on any Select control, not an SAP search feature.

In the long term, submitting an Improvement Request via the SAP Customer Influence portal is the right channel to formally request SAP address the search limitation in this field.

Regards,

Sreevidya

entry.title: Re: Unable to retrieve the price list region-wise or mate...
entry.content: In S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, the standard way to provide price lists is via CDS views and APIs (API_PRICING_CONDITION_SRV), not via legacy reports. For user‑facing analysis, use the Manage Prices – Sales app.

entry.title: Lessons from a Phase 2 SAP SF Rollout:-Governance, migration control, and partner accountability
entry.content:
About This Piece

I had the opportunity to work as the functional and technical Lead consultant on this engagement — a Phase 2 Employee Central and Time Off rollout that extended an already-live SAP SuccessFactors instance to two new countries in South-East Asia. My part in it covered configuration build, migration planning, UAT coordination, Production deployment along with working closely with the client's IT Governance & HR team and an existing AMS partner. It was a good learning experience, and the points below come straight from that project, not from a textbook.

Phase 2 Is a Different Animal

When you build SuccessFactors from scratch, there is always some room to adjust as you go along. Phase 2 does not give you that luxury. The instance is already live, real people are using it in other countries, and a change to a shared object — a Corporate Data Model, a global Business Rule, a Position structure, leave object etc— touches every region on the system, not just the ones you are rolling out to.

A few things make Phase 2 that much more demanding:

  • The system is already running Core HR ESS, MSS process including payroll and leave transactions for live users elsewhere, so mistakes have immediate consequences.
  • Shared objects belong to everyone on the instance, so even a small, well-intentioned change can ripple outward.
  • The stakeholder circle is wider too — IT Governance, AMS, HR teams from multiple countries, and the implementation team all need to know exactly where their role begins and ends.

Four Things Worth Getting Right

1. Lock Governance Early, and Keep It Locked

On this engagement, a bundle-only migration rule was introduced two-thirds of the way through build, without an impact assessment or a timeline review. It cost us roughly two weeks of rework, and a fair bit of back-and-forth to get everyone aligned again. It taught us, quite plainly, that governance decided upfront is worth far more than governance revisited midway.

What we would recommend:

Lock governance, RACI, and the migration approach during Discovery, before a single configuration object is built. If the approach must change later, let it go through a proper impact assessment and sign-off first.

2. Not All Configuration Objects Are the Same

Standard, country-specific configuration moves through a bundle smoothly — you build it, transport it, validate it, done. Shared global objects need a gentler hand. On this project, a Corporate Data Model change, Position object change, leave object change meant for the new countries had to be manually checked against the live configuration already used by three other regions before we could touch it.

What we would recommend:

Use a hybrid migration model. Let standard, country-scoped configuration e.g. country specific data model changes, country specific custom objects, benefits, Leaves etc go through Transport Management as usual, and handle shared global objects manually, with a documented comparison against Production each time.

3. Draw Clear Lines Between Partners

Most Phase 2 programs bring together at least four parties — the Implementation Partner, the HR team, the AMS partner, and the client's IT Governance team. On our project, the AMS partner was set up for support-level work, but ended up in an implementation-grade role e.g. all UAT migration to be done with implementation consultants on call. Our consultants stayed engaged during migration process explaining  migration tracker , bundles, migration fixes, defect fixes, to help out, time that could otherwise have gone into test scripts, user guides, and proper knowledge transfer.

What we would recommend:

Agree on a simple competency matrix upfront. Implementation owns build, migration, and UAT fixes; AMS can shadow from UAT if they are ready for it, and takes over formally from Hypercare onwards.

4. Treat Data Migration as Implementation Work, Not an Admin Task

Data migration deserves the same seriousness as configuration build. On this engagement, ownership of the Production upload shifted twice — first over data privacy concerns, then over readiness —during UAT it was done by AMS partner under the guidance of Implementation consultant. During production migration, process got changed, it was initially given to HR before it settled with the implementation Consultants.

What we would recommend:

Fix data ownership, access, and privacy approvals at least 60 days before Go-Live, and make sure whoever runs the Production upload has already done at least one full mock load in QAS. Please note that Core HR data migration is a significant activity that involves uploading data across more than 20 templates. Therefore, the technical competency of the nominated resource should be thoroughly assessed before deciding whether to assign the activity to the HR team or the AMS partner.

 

A Quick Word on UAT

UAT on a shared instance genuinely needs more care, since QAS is shared too. But care should not mean everything grinds to a halt. On our project, one small, three-field configuration fix took two full days to move through the standard approval cycle. That experience is what led the team to introduce a same-day Emergency Fix Protocol for low-risk defects, with AMS reviewing the change rather than gatekeeping it. It made a real difference to morale during a demanding phase of the project.

What We Learned: Summary

Area

What Happened

What Helped

Governance

Migration approach changed mid-build, with no impact review.

Lock governance and RACI during Discovery; any later change goes through a formal impact assessment.

Migration Strategy

A bundle-only rule was applied even to shared global objects.

Use a hybrid model — standard config via Transport Management, shared objects handled manually.

Partner Roles

AMS was scoped for support but stepped into an implementation-grade role.

Agree a competency matrix upfront; AMS formally takes over from Hypercare.

Data Ownership

Upload ownership shifted twice, quite late in the project.

Lock ownership 60+ days out; require a full QAS mock load before Production. Assess technical competency of the nominated role for Data upload.

UAT Agility

Every fix, big or small, went through the full approval cycle.

Introduce an Emergency Fix Protocol for low-risk defects.

A Final Thought

Phase 2 programs bring their own kind of pressure, but they also bring some of the best team moments — the late evenings sorted out together, the small wins that nobody outside the project ever hears about. Most of what we have shared here is entirely preventable, simply by getting a few things right before execution begins rather than fixing them in the middle of it.

These lessons come from genuinely being in the room when things got a little difficult, and figuring our way through them as a team. If you are heading into a Phase 2 SuccessFactors rollout and want to think through governance, migration, or your partner model, we would be happy to have that conversation. And if you have your own lessons from a similar journey, do share them — that is exactly the kind of thing the SAP community grows stronger from.

Thank you!

Mohammad Saddam Aquib

entry.title: Power BI Refresh Failure When S/4HANA Is Unavailable Despite Persisted Data in SAP Datasphere
entry.content:
We have the following data flow:

SAP S/4HANA (On-Premise) → SAP Datasphere (CDS Remote Table) → Persisted Data → Power BI

The data from the CDS Remote Table has been successfully persisted in SAP Datasphere. However, when the SAP S/4HANA source system is unavailable, the Power BI dataset refresh fails.

Expected Behavior:
Since the data is already persisted in SAP Datasphere, Power BI should be able to refresh successfully by reading the persisted data without requiring connectivity to the SAP S/4HANA source system.

Actual Behavior:
When SAP S/4HANA is unavailable, the Power BI refresh fails, indicating that there is still a dependency on the source system even though the data has been persisted.

Request:
Please help us understand:

* Why does the Power BI refresh still depend on SAP S/4HANA after the data has been persisted in SAP Datasphere?
* Is this expected behavior for persisted data based on CDS Remote Tables?

entry.title: Re: Sales Order to AR Invoice query
entry.content:
SELECT DISTINCT

T0.DocEntry,

CASE
WHEN T2.DocDate IS NOT NULL
THEN CAST(YEAR(T2.DocDate) AS VARCHAR(4)) + '-' +
RIGHT(CAST(YEAR(T2.DocDate)+1 AS VARCHAR(4)),2)
ELSE ''
END AS [Invoice FY],

T4.SeriesName,
T0.DocNum AS [SO No],
T0.DocDate AS [SO Date],
T0.CardCode as [Customer Code],
T0.CardName as [Customer Name],

CASE T0.U_Type
WHEN 1 THEN 'Sales'
WHEN 2 THEN 'Service'
WHEN 3 THEN 'Blank'
WHEN 4 THEN 'Adv-PO-Sales'
WHEN 5 THEN 'Adv-PO-Service'
WHEN 6 THEN 'Sales - Life Sciences'
WHEN 7 THEN 'Service - Life Sciences'
WHEN 8 THEN 'Sales - FD'
WHEN 9 THEN 'Service - FD'
WHEN 10 THEN 'Sales - MSF'
WHEN 11 THEN 'Service - MSF'
END AS'Type',

T0.U_Region as [Region],
(T0.DocTotal-T0.VatSum) as 'SO Total',

CASE
WHEN T1.LineStatus = 'O' AND T0.CANCELED = 'N'
THEN (SELECT Sum([LineTotal]) FROM RDR1 WHERE DocEntry = T0.DocEntry AND LineStatus='O') ELSE 0 END AS [Open SO Amount],

CASE
WHEN T1.LineStatus = 'C' AND T0.CANCELED = 'N'
THEN (SELECT Sum([LineTotal]) FROM RDR1 WHERE DocEntry = T0.DocEntry AND LineStatus='C') ELSE 0 END AS [Closed SO Amount],

CASE
WHEN T0.CANCELED = 'Y'
THEN (T0.DocTotal-T0.VatSum) ELSE 0 END AS [Cancelled SO Amount],

--CASE
-- WHEN T0.CANCELED = 'Y' THEN 'Cancelled'
-- WHEN T0.DocStatus = 'C' THEN 'Closed'
-- WHEN T0.DocStatus = 'O' THEN 'Open'
--ELSE 'Unknown'END AS [SO Status],

T2.DocNum AS [Invoice No],

--CASE
-- WHEN T2.CANCELED = 'N'
-- THEN Sum((T3.[Price]*T3.Quantity))
--END As 'Inv Total'

T2.DocDate AS [Invoice Date],
(T2.DocTotal-T2.VatSum) as 'INV Total'

--CASE
-- WHEN T2.CANCELED = 'Y' THEN 'Cancelled'
-- WHEN T2.DocStatus = 'C' THEN 'Closed'
-- WHEN T2.DocStatus = 'O' THEN 'Open'
--ELSE 'Unknown'END AS [Invoice Status]



FROM ORDR T0
INNER JOIN RDR1 T1 ON T0.DocEntry = T1.DocEntry
LEFT OUTER JOIN dln1 T3A ON T1.DocEntry = T3A.BaseEntry AND T1.LineNum = T3A.BaseLine AND T3A.ItemCode=T1.ItemCode
LEFT OUTER JOIN INV1 T3 ON T3."BaseEntry" = (case when T3."BaseType"='17' then T1.DocEntry when T3."BaseType"='15' then T3A.DocEntry else 0 end)
AND T3."BaseLine" = (case when T3."BaseType"='17' then T1.LineNum else T3A.LineNum end)
AND T3."ItemCode" = (case when T3."BaseType"='17' then T1.ItemCode else T3A.ItemCode end)
LEFT OUTER JOIN OINV T2 ON T3.DocEntry = T2.DocEntry
LEFT outer JOIN NNM1 T4 ON T0.Series = T4.Series
LEFT JOIN RDN1 T5 ON T5.BaseType = 15 AND T5.BaseEntry = T3A.DocEntry AND T5.BaseLine = T3A.LineNum

Where
--T0.DocDate BETWEEN [%0] AND [%1] and (T2.docDate IS NULL OR T2.DocDate <= [%1])
T0.DocEntry = '71380'
--and T5.DocEntry IS NULL
--and T0.CANCELED = 'Y'
--and (T2.CANCELED = 'N')

Group By T1.Price,
T1.Quantity,
T3.[Price],T3.Quantity,
T1.DiscPrcnt,
T0.DocNum,
T0.DocDate,
T2.DocNum,
T2.DocDate,
T0.CANCELED,
T2.CANCELED,
T0.DocStatus,
T2.DocStatus,
T0.CardCode,
T0.CardName,
T0.DocEntry,
T4.SeriesName,
T0.DocTotal,
T0.VatSum,
T1.LineTotal,
T1.LineStatus,
T2.DocTotal,
T2.VatSum


ORDER BY
T0.DocDate,
T0.DocNum,
T0.CardCode

 

 

Regards,

Aagam S.

entry.title: Can SAP API Management directly invoke SAP S/4HANA OData services without SAP Cloud Integration?
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I have an architectural question regarding the use of SAP API Management and would appreciate your insights.

Scenario

I have a client application that sends data to an API Proxy in SAP API Management. Instead of forwarding the request to an SAP Cloud Integration (CPI) iFlow, I am considering calling an SAP S/4HANA OData service directly from the API Proxy.

Since the target OData service requires an X-CSRF token for POST operations, the API Proxy would need to perform the following steps:

  1. Receive the incoming request from the client.
  2. Fetch the X-CSRF token from the SAP S/4HANA OData service.
  3. Reuse the token (and cookies, if required) to execute the POST request.
  4. Return the response back to the client.

My Questions

  1. Is this architecture technically feasible using only SAP API Management, without involving SAP Cloud Integration (CPI)?
  2. Can SAP API Management reliably handle the complete X-CSRF token flow (token retrieval, cookie handling, and POST request)?
  3. Is this approach recommended for production environments, or are there any technical limitations or best practices that should be considered?
  4. What would be the major drawbacks compared to using SAP Cloud Integration, particularly regarding:
    • Error handling
    • Retry mechanisms
    • Monitoring and logging
    • Security
    • Scalability
    • Maintainability

Why I'm Asking

The motivation behind this approach is to optimize SAP Cloud Integration message consumption and potentially reduce licensing costs for simple pass-through scenarios. If API Management can securely and reliably handle such integrations, it could eliminate the need for an iFlow in certain use cases.

Has anyone implemented a similar architecture in a production environment? If so, I would appreciate hearing about your experience and whether you would recommend this approach.

Thank you in advance for your guidance!

entry.title: S/4 HANA BRF+: In Sales Order system is triggering the output with Channel = Print instead of Email?
entry.content:
Dear All,

In S/4HANA BRF+, I’m facing a challenge: in change mode of Sales Order, the system is triggering the output with Channel = Print instead of Email for output type: ORDER_CONFIRMATION_CHANGE

Scenario:

  1. Sales Order Creation (VA01), Sales Org: UK01, Order Type: ZOR, Shipper ST maintained in PA40, Requested Delivery Date: 07.07.2026, Quantity: 1, Schedule Line Date: 07.07.2026, Confirmed Qty: 1, Save → Output Type ORDER_CONFIRMATION triggered successfully.
  2. Sales Order Change (VA02), Requested Delivery Date: 31.07.2026, Quantity: 2, Schedule Line Date: 31.07.2026, Confirmed Qty: 2, Save → Output Type ORDER_CONFIRMATION_CHANGE triggered with Channel = Print instead of EMAIL

Requirement: Whenever there is a change in delivery date or quantity, the system should:

  • Trigger ORDER_CONFIRMATION_CHANGE output automatically with channel EMAIL.

We have maintained the below rules:

show rules for - SALES DOCUMENT

Determination Set

output type - (Document Cat. =C, Sales Org.=UK01, Sales Document type <>BV exclude =SO01, =CCIS(Consignment Issue), Output Type VA01 - Sales Order created with Requested Delivery date - 07.07.2026, Qty-1, Schedule line date - 07.07.2026, confirmed qty = 1 save, Dispatch Time - 1 (Immediate)

Receiver - Output type =ORDER_CONFIRMATION_CHANGE, Role - ST, Exclusive Indicator -(False)

Channel - Output type =ORDER_CONFIRMATION_CHANGE, Sales Org - UK01, Role = ST, Recipient (Blank), Channel - EMAIL, Exclusive Indicator -(False)

Printer Setting - Output type =ORDER_CONFIRMATION_CHANGE, Role (Blank). Printer Queue - ZPDF, Number of Copies - 1

Email Setting - Output type =ORDER_CONFIRMATION_CHANGE,Role =ST, Sales Org =UK01, Sender Email - UK_GS@gcs, Email Template - ZZ_SD_SLS_Default_Email_SO

Email Recipient - Output type =ORDER_CONFIRMATION_CHANGE, Recipient (Blank), Sales Org=UK01, Email Type Code -TO, Email Address Paula@GcS.com

Form Template - Output type =ORDER_CONFIRMATION_CHANGE,Company Code to be billed = UK01, Sales Org (Blank), Role=ST, Channel=Email, Sender Country (Blank), Receipient City/Region (Blank), Recipient Language (Blank), Form Template - ZO2C_OC_ORDER_CONF, Form Language - English

Could you please advise why the system is triggering the output with Channel = Print instead of Email?

Thankyou.

Dear customer,

In order to see all templates in the Service Request application, the S-user authorization ''Create Billable Service Requests" needs to be assigned on customer number or CCC level (only installation number level will not show all templates). 

Could you pls kindly request the mentioned authorization, use the Self-Service Request (KBA 2891528) or contact your Super administrator directly?

Also refer to KBA 3463116 - Missing template(s) when creating service request - SAP for Me.

Hope this helps.

Thanks and regards,
SAP Support

We’re excited to welcome you to the SAP ACC APAC  Digital hub throughout and after the event!

What can you use this space for?

This group is designed to help you stay connected and get the most out of your conference experience. Here’s how you can use it:

  • Ask questions anytime – before, during, and after sessions

While this Digital Hub is a great starting point, we encourage you to explore the following:

Group Area -  Connect & Exchange 

A space for interaction among community members, where you can connect, discuss, and share your teaching experiences with peers and experts.

Topic Page - Your Learning Overview

A dedicated page providing a holistic overview of SAP University Alliances program teaching and learning offerings, including learning paths, academic lessons, quick start guides, and testimonials.

Blog - Earn your SAP ACC Digital Badge!

Take the opportunity to earn your conference digital badge by replying to this blog. Just let us know if you were an attendee or speaker.

We encourage you to actively participate, ask questions, and engage with others. Let’s make this a collaborative and insightful experience for everyone!

entry.title: Displaying Actual PR Final Approval Date in ME5A Report
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

The release date in the PR is currently determined based on the formula (Delivery Date – GR Processing Time). If the GR Processing Time is not maintained in the Material Master, the system uses the value maintained in the Plant Parameters configuration.

However, our requirements are different. They need the actual PR release date, which is the date on which the Purchase Requisition was finally approved by the final approvers, instead of the system-calculated release date  and same needs to be reflected in ME5A report .

Kindly suggest whether there is any feasible way to achieve this requirement.

entry.title: Business Data Architecture: Transformations - Making Architectural Boundaries Enforceable
entry.content:
Understanding architecture is one thing. Enforcing it is another.

You understand the concepts - modeling layers organize data flow, data product types structure reuse, platforms own domains. But when a developer asks: "Can I add Revenue Growth % to my Definition layer data product?" - how do you answer?

Without explicit rules, the answer is judgment: "Probably not, that feels like semantic layer logic." With 10 developers making judgment calls, you get 10 different interpretations. Architectural boundaries dissolve.

Transformations make boundaries enforceable. They classify data transformations and specify which layers and data product types each can be used in. The question "Can I add Revenue Growth % to Definition Layer?" gets a concrete answer: "No. That's KPI Calculation - only allowed in Semantic Layer."

This isn't about one developer building one data product. It's about 50 developers across 5 platforms making consistent decisions over 3 years. The architecture stays intact because the rules are explicit, not implicit.

This post defines the transformation framework. It establishes which transformations are allowed in which scope (layer + data product type), provides the catalog structure for organizing them, and includes the process for defining new transformations. Let's start with what transformations actually are in Business Data Architecture.

Transformations

Transformations are the mechanism that makes the framework enforceable. They're logical patterns (not platform-specific code) that define what kinds of change can happen to data - categorized into two types based on their scope.

Two Transformation Categories

1. Business Transformations - Layer-specific

  • These transformations implement business logic - changing data meaning, structure, or interpretation based on business rules. Each belongs to specific layers based on its purpose. Examples: Currency Conversion, KPI Calculation, Join Across Sources, Data Type Normalization.

2. Operational Transformations - Allowed everywhere

  • These transformations support observability, audit trails, and operational metadata without changing business meaning. They're allowed in any layer because they track operational state, not business logic. Examples: Add Load Timestamp, Add Lineage Metadata, Add Process ID, Add Data Quality Metrics.

Transformation Anatomy

Whether operational or business, every transformation is defined using the same six-part structure. This consistency ensures that anyone in your organization can look at a transformation definition and immediately understand what it does, where it belongs, and what rules apply.

Here's what every transformation contains:

This structure makes transformations complete and actionable - you know what it does, where you can use it, and what rules you must follow.

What Transformations Are NOT

You now understand the anatomy - six elements that define any transformation. But before we show you what a populated transformation catalog looks like, there's a critical distinction to establish: transformations are logical patterns that describe change, not the code that executes it. Here's what that means:

Transformations are logical patterns that describe WHAT change happens to data. They are NOT:

  • Artifacts - Not SQL views, dbt models, Python scripts, Databricks notebooks, or any platform-specific code
  • Implementation details - Not HOW the change is executed, just WHAT change occurs
  • Platform-specific - The same transformation (e.g., "Currency Conversion") can be implemented differently on Snowflake, SAP Datasphere, or Databricks

Example: "Currency Conversion" is a transformation - a logical pattern. It might be implemented as a Snowflake SQL view, a dbt model, or a Python notebook. The transformation defines WHAT (convert currency values), WHERE (which layers), and WHAT LIMITS apply (constraints). The artifact is HOW it's actually executed.

The relationship between transformations and artifacts is covered in our next post on artifact roles - which artifacts can execute which transformations.

You now understand what transformations are: logical patterns with six elements, categorized as operational or business, distinct from the code that implements them. But understanding the concept isn't enough - everyone needs to reference the same definitions.

The Transformation Catalog

How do you ensure 50 developers across multiple platforms all use the same transformation definitions?

A central catalog - your repository of defined transformations that serves as the single source of truth. Without it, each team invents their own "Data Masking" or "Currency Conversion" - same names, different implementations, inconsistent results.

The catalog could be a wiki, a repository, or a metadata system. What matters is that it's:

  • Centralized - One place, not scattered across teams
  • Accessible - All developers can find and reference it
  • Governed - Changes go through a formal process
  • Versioned - Track when transformations are added or deprecated

Catalog Structure

Here's what a populated catalog looks like, organized by layer and category. This is NOT exhaustive - these are examples showing structure.

 

Key observations:

  • Ingestion layer: Not shown because it uses only operational transformations - preserving source data without business logic
  • Define/Apply pattern: Reference data products define mappings once (e.g., Define Source Metric), and other data products apply them in their specific layers (e.g., Apply Source Metric Definition in Definition layer)
  • Multi-layer transformations: Some business transformations appear in multiple layers with different purposes. For example, Access Control appears in both Semantic layer (row-level security for analytical data products) and Sharing layer (consumption-level security for all exposed data)

This catalog gives you a shared vocabulary and consistent patterns across your organization. But what happens when you need a transformation that doesn't exist yet?

Adding New Transformations

Adding a new transformation requires formal definition and communication to ensure consistent reuse across teams.

This five-step process ensures every transformation in your organization follows the same structure, gets reviewed for consistency, and becomes discoverable to all teams. It's the difference between 50 developers each inventing their own "Currency Conversion" and 50 developers reusing one well-defined transformation.

The transformation framework gives you the governance mechanism to keep architecture intact at scale. Transformations define what's allowed where, the catalog organizes them, and the process ensures they're defined consistently.

You now have the complete transformation framework: two categories (operational and business), six-element anatomy, a catalog structure to organize them, and a process to add new ones. This framework makes architectural boundaries enforceable - turning abstract concepts like "layer separation" into concrete rules developers can follow.

Equally important: transformations give you a common language across platforms. When a Snowflake developer and a Datasphere developer both implement "Currency Conversion," they're following the same logical definition - same purpose, same constraints, different code. This prevents business logic from fragmenting into platform-specific interpretations. One architectural definition, multiple platform implementations, consistent behavior.

What's next

Transformations define what logic belongs where (layer + data product type). But how do you ensure a SQL view executes only the transformations it's allowed to? Or that a stored procedure doesn't violate layer boundaries?

This is where Artifact Roles come in. You know what artifacts are - SQL views, stored procedures, graphical views. But not all artifacts can execute all transformations. The next post introduces roles that constrain which transformations an artifact can execute, ensuring your platform implementations respect the architectural boundaries you just defined.

This blog post is part of the Business Data Architecture for Modern Analytics Landscapes series.

⬅️  Previous Post | Next Post  ➡️
